name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَجَعَلُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ جُزْءًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
```

```english
They ascribe to Him offspring from among His servants! Man is indeed a manifest ingrate. (15)
```

```arabic
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَنَاتٍ وَأَصْفَىٰكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ (١٦)
```

```english
Did He adopt daughters from what He creates while He preferred you with sons? (16)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِمَا ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مَثَلًا ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ (١٧)
```

```english
When one of them is brought the news of what he ascribes to the All-beneficent, his face becomes darkened and he chokes with suppressed rage, [and says] (17)
```

```arabic
أَوَمَن يُنَشَّؤُا۟ فِى ٱلْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْخِصَامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ (١٨)
```

```english
‘What! One who is brought up amid ornaments and is inconspicuous in contests?’ (18)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبَـٰدُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ أَشَهِدُوا۟ خَلْقَهُمْ ۚ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهَـٰدَتُهُمْ وَيُسْـَٔلُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
They have made the angels—who are servants of the All-beneficent—females. Were they witness to their creation? Their testimony will be written down and they shall be questioned. (19)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَا عَبَدْنَـٰهُم ۗ مَّا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They do not have any knowledge of that, and they do nothing but surmise. (20)
```

```arabic
أَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ فَهُم بِهِۦ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
Did We give them a Book before this, so that they are holding fast to it? (21)
```

```arabic
بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
No, they said, ‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided in their footsteps.’ (22)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّقْتَدُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
So it has been that We did not send any warner to a town before you, without its affluent ones saying, ‘We found our fathers following a creed and we are indeed following in their footsteps.’ (23)
```

```arabic
۞ قَـٰلَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُم بِأَهْدَىٰ مِمَّا وَجَدتُّمْ عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
He would say, ‘What! Even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?!’ They would say, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you are sent with.’ (24)
```

```arabic
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٥)
```

```english
Thereupon We took vengeance on them; so observe how was the fate of the deniers. (25)
```

## (Commentary)

This section recounts some of their claims, to which they were driven by excess and ingratitude for blessings (الكفر بالنعم)—namely, their claim that Allah has offspring and that the angels are the daughters of Allah, the Glorified. It also addresses their arguments for worshipping the angels and provides a rebuttal against them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلُوا لَهُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ جُزْءاً إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَكَفُورٌ مُبِينٌ** — "They attribute to Him a part of His servants. Indeed man is a manifest ingrate." (43:15). What is meant by "part" (جزءاً) is offspring, for procreation is nothing but derivation (الاشتقاق); thus, the offspring is a part of its parent, separated from him and fashioned after his form.

The offspring was expressed as a "part" (الجزء) to point toward the impossibility of their claim. This is because the partiality of one thing from another, however it is conceived, cannot be realized except through composition (التركب) within that thing, whereas Allah, the Glorified, is One (واحد) from every aspect.

It has become clear from what preceded that the phrase "of His servants" (مِنْ عِبادِهِ) serves as an exposition (بيان) for His saying "a part" (جزءاً). There is no harm in this type of exposition preceding the thing being expounded (المبين), nor in the exposition being plural while the thing being expounded is singular.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمِ اتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَناتٍ وَأَصْفاكُمْ بِالْبَنِينَ** — "Has He taken daughters from what He creates and favored you with sons?" (43:16). That is, He has reserved sons exclusively for you.

Thus, you have sons while He has nothing but daughters, even though you consider the daughter to be inferior to the son. You attribute to Him the inferior of the two categories while specializing yourselves with the more noble of them. This, in addition to being an impossible claim in itself, constitutes a manifest disparagement, insult, and ingratitude.

The restriction of "taking daughters" to being "from what He creates" (مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ) is because they claimed that the angels—despite their lordship and divinity—were created by Allah. The shift (الالتفات) in the verse to addressing them directly is to emphasize the obligation (الإلزام) and to establish the rebuke. The use of the indefinite for "daughters" (بنات) and the definite for "the sons" (البنين) is for the purpose of belittlement and aggrandizement, respectively.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُمْ بِما ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمنِ مَثَلاً ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ** — "When one of them is brought the news of [the birth of] what he ascribes to the All-beneficent as a likeness, his face becomes darkened and he is choked with grief." (43:17). The **likeness** (المثل) is the peer and the similar thing that is of the same genus as something else. To "strike a likeness" (ضرب الشيء مثلاً) for something is to take it as being of the same genus. The phrase "of what he ascribes to the All-beneficent as a likeness" (بِما ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمنِ مَثَلاً) refers to the female. The **grieved** (الكظيم) is one filled with distress and rage.

The meaning is: Their state is such that when one of them is given the news of a female—whom he has made a similar peer to the All-Beneficent—his face turns black from sorrow, and he is filled with distress and rage because they do not accept that for themselves and consider it a disgrace, yet they accept it for Him.

The shift (الالتفات) in the verse to the third person (الغيبة) is to recount their hideous conduct and ugly way to others so that they may marvel at it.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَمَنْ يُنَشَّؤُا فِي الْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِي الْخِصامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ** — "What! One who is brought up amid ornaments and who is non-demonstrative in disputes?" (43:18). That is, have they assigned to Allah, the Glorified, one who is brought up in ornaments (الحلية)—meaning raised in finery—and who, in disputes and argumentation, is unable to make their proof clear (غير مبين) or establish their claim?

These two attributes were mentioned because a woman, by nature, possesses stronger emotion and compassion and weaker intellect (التعقل) in comparison to a man, whereas the man is the opposite. Among the clearest manifestations of the strength of her emotions is her intense attachment to ornaments and finery, and her weakness in establishing an argument, which is based on the strength of intellect.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً** — "They have made the angels, who are servants of the All-beneficent, females..." (43:19). This is the meaning of their saying that the angels are the daughters of Allah. Groups among the pre-Islamic Arabs used to say this. As for other idolaters (الوثنية), they might have included among their deities a goddess who was the mother of a god or the daughter of a god, but they did not claim that all angels were females, as is apparent from what is recounted in the Noble Verse.

The angels were described by His saying: "who are servants of the All-beneficent" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ) as a rebuttal to the claim of their femininity, because the term "servants" (العباد) in this masculine form is not applied to females. This does not necessitate that they are characterized by masculinity (الذكورة) in the sense that an animal is characterized, for the masculinity and femininity found in animals are among the requirements of their material existence (الوجود المادي) equipped for procreation and the production of likenesses, whereas the angels are far removed from that.

His saying: **أَشَهِدُوا خَلْقَهُمْ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهادَتُهُمْ وَيُسْئَلُونَ** — "Did they witness their creation? Their testimony will be written down and they will be questioned." (43:19). This is a rebuttal of their claim of femininity in the angels by stating that the path to such knowledge is through the senses, yet they have not seen them to know this. They were not present at their creation to witness it.

Thus, His saying: "Did they witness their creation..." is a rhetorical question of denial (استفهام إنكاري) and a threat for speaking without knowledge. That is, they did not witness their creation, and this testimony of theirs will be written in the records of their deeds against them, and they will be questioned about it on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ** — "They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They have no knowledge of that; they do nothing but conjecture." (43:20). This is a fallacious rational argument recounted from them, which can be formulated in two ways:

First, to establish the validity of worshipping partners (الشركاء) by saying: "If Allah had willed that we not worship the partners, we would not have worshipped them, by necessity, because it is impossible for His intended act to deviate from His will (إرادته). However, we do worship them; therefore, He did not will [that we stop], and the absence of His will for us to stop is an authorization (إذن) for our worship. Thus, there is no prohibition from Allah, the Exalted, against worshipping the partners, and the angels are among them." This meaning is what comes to mind from His saying in Surah al-An'am: **سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "The polytheists will say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have ascribed any partner [to Him], nor our fathers, nor would we have forbidden anything.’" (6:148), as indicated by the context before and after it.

Second, it can be formulated to invalidate prophethood (النبوة), which claims that Allah makes such-and-such obligatory upon you and forbids such-and-such. This is done by saying: "If Allah had willed that we not worship partners, nor permit or forbid anything, we would not have worshipped partners nor established any ruling from ourselves, because it is impossible for His intended act to deviate from His will. However, we do worship them and we do permit and forbid things; therefore, Allah, the Glorified, has not willed anything from us. Thus, the claim that Allah commands you with this and forbids you from that—and in short, that He willed such-and-such—is false."

This meaning is the apparent one understood from His saying, the Exalted, in Surah al-Nahl: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers—nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him.’" (16:35), considering the context.

Their saying in the recounted verse under discussion: "Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them" (لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ), according to what the context of the preceding and succeeding verses provides, is intended as an argument for the first meaning—which is the justification of their worship of the angels. Thus, it carries the same meaning as the verse in Surah al-An'am, though more specific.

His saying: "They have no knowledge of that" (ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ) means that this is a statement from them based on ignorance. It is a sophistry (مغالطة) in which they confused the creative will (الإرادة التكوينية) — *the irresistible decree of Allah by which He brings things into existence* — with the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية) — *the commands and prohibitions Allah sets for His servants to follow by their own choice* — and took the former in place of the latter. The implication of the argument is only that there is no creative will from Him, the Exalted, pertaining to their non-worship of the angels. However, the absence of this type of will regarding their non-worship does not necessitate the absence of the legislative will regarding it.

Since He, the Glorified, did not will that they not worship partners through the creative will, they remained autonomous (مختارين), not compelled (غير مضطرين) to act or refrain. He then willed from them, through the legislative will, that they maintain monotheism (التوحيد) and not worship partners. It is not impossible for the intended act to deviate from the legislative will because it is conventional (اعتبارية), not ontological (حقيقية). It is only used in laws, regulations, and master-servant obligations (التكاليف المولوية), and the reality upon which it is based is the act's inclusion of a benefit (مصلحة) or a corruption (مفسدة).

From what has preceded, the flaw in the following claim becomes apparent: "Their argument is based on two premises: first, that their worship of the angels is by the will of Allah, the Exalted; and second, that this necessitates it being pleasing to Him. They were correct in the first and erred in the second, as they were ignorant that the will (المشية) consists of preferring some possibilities over others, whatever they may be, without considering pleasure or displeasure in either side."

The reason this is flawed is that the content of the argument is the absence of the will pertaining to the abandonment of worship. The absence of the will to abandon does not necessitate the presence of the will to act; rather, its consequence is permission (الإذن), which is the absence of prohibition from the act. Furthermore, the proponent of this view restricted "will" to the creative and neglected the legislative, upon which master-servant obligations revolve, and this is an error on his part.

Also apparent is the flaw in what has been attributed to some: that the meaning of their saying, "Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them," is an excuse for worshipping the angels by attributing it to Allah's will, while admitting it is ugly.

This is because they did not concede the ugliness of worshipping their deities such that they would offer an excuse for it. Indeed, it is recounted from them later: "We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided by their footsteps."

His saying: "they do nothing but conjecture" (إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ). **Conjecture** (الخرص)—according to what appears from al-Raghib—is speaking based on opinion and guesswork; it has also been interpreted as lying.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً مِنْ قَبْلِهِ فَهُمْ بِهِ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ** — "Have We given them a Book before this, so that they are holding fast to it?" (43:21). The pronoun in "before this" (مِنْ قَبْلِهِ) refers to the Quran. The verse denies that they have any proof from the path of transmission (النقل), just as the previous verse denied their proof from the path of reason (العقل). The sum of the two verses is that they have no proof for worshipping the angels, neither through reason nor through transmission; thus, Allah has not authorized it.

His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ قالُوا إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلى آثارِهِمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — "Rather they say, ‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided by their footsteps.’" (43:22). The **creed** (الأمة) is the way that is aimed for and intended; what is meant by it is religion (الدين). This is a digression (إضراب) from what was concluded by the two verses. The meaning is: They have no evidence for the truth of their worship; rather, they said, "We found our fathers following a religion and we are guided by their footsteps." That is, they are merely clinging to the imitation (تقليد) of their fathers.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَذلِكَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ إِلَّا قالَ مُتْرَفُوها إِنَّا وَجَدْنا** — "And so it has been that We did not send any warner to a town before you but its affluent ones said, ‘We found...’" (43:23). That is, clinging to the hem of imitation is not something exclusive to these people; rather, it was the habit of their predecessors among the polytheistic nations. We did not send before you to any town a warner (نذير)—who is the Prophet—but that its people of luxury and ease clung to the hem of imitation and said: "We found our predecessors following a religion, and we are following in their footsteps; we shall not abandon it nor oppose them."

The attribution of this statement to their "affluent ones" (مترفيهم) is to indicate that luxury and ease are what drive them toward imitation and divert them from contemplating the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِأَهْدى مِمَّا وَجَدْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ آباءَكُمْ** — "He said, ‘What! Even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?’" (43:24). The speaker is the warner, and the address is to the affluent ones, including others by extension. The conjunction in "What! Even if I bring you" (أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُمْ) is linked to a deleted phrase indicated by their speech. The estimation is: "Are you following in their footsteps even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?" The warner's description of what he brought them as "better guidance" (أهدى) than their religion—despite their religion being false with no guidance in it—is a matter of engaging the opponent on their own terms (مجاراة الخصم).

And their saying: **قالُوا إِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ** — "They said, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you have been sent with.’" (43:24). This is their response to the warner's saying: "What! Even if I bring you...", and it is an arbitrary assertion (تحكم) without proof.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ** — "So We took vengeance on them. So observe how was the end of the deniers!" (43:25). That is, as a consequence of that sending [of messengers] and the rejection through imitation and arbitrary assertion, We destroyed them for their denial. So look at how the end was for those predecessors among the people of the towns. In this is a threat to the people of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.
